coredata.dc:title: A facile, sensitive, and highly specific trinitrophenol assay based on target-induced synergetic effects of acid induction and electron transfer towards DNA-templated copper nanoclusters
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Haiyin
Chang, Jiafu
Hou, Ting
Ge, Lei
Li, Feng
coredata.dc:description: Reliable, selective and sensitive approaches for trinitrophenol (TNP) detection are highly desirable with respect to national security and environmental protection. Herein, a simple and novel fluorescent strategy for highly sensitive and specific TNP assay has been successfully developed, which is based on the quenching of the fluorescent poly(thymine)-templated copper nanoclusters (DNA-CuNCs), through the synergetic effects of acid induction and electron transfer. Upon the addition of TNP, donor-acceptor complexes between the electron-deficient nitro-groups in TNP and the electron-donating DNA templates are formed, resulting in the close proximity between TNP and CuNCs. Moreover, the acidity of TNP contributes to the pH decrease of the system. These factors combine to dramatically quench the fluorescence of DNA-CuNCs, providing a “signal-off” strategy for TNP sensing. The as-proposed strategy demonstrates high sensitivity for TNP assay, and a detection limit of 0.03μM is obtained, which is lower than those reported by using organic fluorescent materials. More significantly, this approach shows outstanding selectivity over a number of TNP analogues, such as 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT), 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP), 3-nitrophenol (NP), nitrobenzene (NB), phenol (BP), and toluene (BT). Compared with previous studies, this method does not need complex DNA sequence design, fluorescent dye labeling, or sophisticated organic reactions, rendering the strategy with additional advantages of simplicity and cost-effectiveness. In addition, the as-proposed strategy has been adopted for the detection of TNP in natural water samples, indicating its great potential to be applied in the fields of public safety and environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
Acid induction
Electron transfer
Trinitrophenol
Quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of TNP in water samples
Principle of fluorescence TNP biosensing strategy
Feasibility study of fluorescent TNP assay
Application of TNP sensor in water samples
Mechanism for selective response of DNA-CuNCs to TNP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra under different conditions: (a) DNA-CuNCs; (b) DNA-CuNCs in the presence of TNP. Conditions: [DNA]=0.5μM; [Cu2+]=200μM; [sodium ascorbate]=2.0mM; [TNP]=500μM; pH 7.4, MOPS buffer.

(A) Fluorescence spectra corresponding to the analysis of TNP with different concentrations; (B) The linear plot of FL intensity versus the TNP concentration ranging from 0.1 to 100μM. Inset: the expanded graph with TNP concentration ranging from 0.1 to 1μM. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three repetitive measurements.

Comparison of the FL intensity of DNA-CuNCs in aqueous solutions in the presence of TNP, TNT, DNT, DNP, NP, NB, BP, and BT, respectively, in which “Control” indicates the condition in the absence of explosives. The concentrations of TNP and TNT were 500μM and 800μM, respectively, and the concentrations of other analytes were 1000μM. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three repetitive measurements.
TEM images of DNA-CuNCs before (A) and after (B) the introduction of TNP, whose concentration was 500μM.
The principle of DNA-CuNCs-based fluorescent assay for TNP.
Detection of TNP spiked in water samples (n=3).

head.ce:title: A facile, sensitive, and highly specific trinitrophenol assay based on target-induced synergetic effects of acid induction and electron transfer towards DNA-templated copper nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao, 266109 People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: H.Y. Li and J.F. Chang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reliable, selective and sensitive approaches for trinitrophenol (TNP) detection are highly desirable with respect to national security and environmental protection. Herein, a simple and novel fluorescent strategy for highly sensitive and specific TNP assay has been successfully developed, which is based on the quenching of the fluorescent poly(thymine)-templated copper nanoclusters (DNA-CuNCs), through the synergetic effects of acid induction and electron transfer. Upon the addition of TNP, donor-acceptor complexes between the electron-deficient nitro-groups in TNP and the electron-donating DNA templates are formed, resulting in the close proximity between TNP and CuNCs. Moreover, the acidity of TNP contributes to the pH decrease of the system. These factors combine to dramatically quench the fluorescence of DNA-CuNCs, providing a “signal-off” strategy for TNP sensing. The as-proposed strategy demonstrates high sensitivity for TNP assay, and a detection limit of 0.03μM is obtained, which is lower than those reported by using organic fluorescent materials. More significantly, this approach shows outstanding selectivity over a number of TNP analogues, such as 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT), 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP), 3-nitrophenol (NP), nitrobenzene (NB), phenol (BP), and toluene (BT). Compared with previous studies, this method does not need complex DNA sequence design, fluorescent dye labeling, or sophisticated organic reactions, rendering the strategy with additional advantages of simplicity and cost-effectiveness. In addition, the as-proposed strategy has been adopted for the detection of TNP in natural water samples, indicating its great potential to be applied in the fields of public safety and environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitroaromatic compounds (NACs) are a class of electron deficient compounds, known as explosive materials, and have attracted great attention in recent years due to their military applications, threats of terrorism, and high toxicity to the environment [1]. Thus, rapid, sensitive, and selective detection of NACs is highly desirable. Until now, a variety of methods for NACs detection have been reported, such as mass spectrometry [2], electrochemistry [3], gas chromatography [4], and others. However, most of these techniques are not easily accessible due to the requirements for bulky equipment, relative high cost, time consuming procedure, and complicated manipulations.

Fluorescent chemical sensors, widely used for the detection of various targets and easily applied to fabricate microelectronic devices, are promising optical platforms for simple, sensitive, and selective detection of NACs [5–7]. For fluorescence sensing of NACs, the most frequently used approach is the fluorescence quenching protocol, which is based on the formation of electron-rich fluorescent materials (easily reacted with proton-donating NACs), such as carbon dots [8], organic emitting materials [9], fluorescent polymer compounds [10], and metal-organic architectures [11]. However, the aforementioned electron-rich materials are susceptible to some limitations hampering their application in NACs detection, including the sophisticated organic reaction, weak luminescence, high toxicity, and poor selectivity among NACs [12,13]. To solve such problems, some researchers have reported novel fluorescent sensing materials to achieve simple, sensitive, and selective detection of NACs [14–16]. For instance, Dong's group reported a fluorescent platform for 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) analysis based on aqueous carbon dots [14]. Xia et al. developed a strategy by intercalating ruthenium polypyridine complexes into interlayer galleries of layered double hydroxides to fabricate optical molecular material for TNT assay [16]. However, most research on NACs is primarily directed towards the detection of TNT, and less attention has been paid to trinitrophenol (TNP), whose explosive power is superior to that of its well-known counterpart TNT [17], and the present TNP sensing protocols cannot distinguish TNP from other NACs, especially TNT. Furthermore, as compared to other NACs, TNP possessed high solubility in water to easily contaminate soil and groundwater, resulting in serious environmental pollution [18]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop new sensing materials with simple synthetic processes, excellent luminescence, good stability, and good selectivity for TNP recognition.

Copper nanoclusters have emerged as a novel class of fluorescent probes and among them fluorescent DNA-templated copper nanoclusters (DNA-CuNCs) especially have captured widespread attention [19]. Compared to organic emitting materials and semiconductor dots, DNA-CuNCs are well suitable for the development of fluorescent biosensors due to their outstanding properties, including easy preparation, favorable optical property, high photostability, low toxicity, and long analytical wavelength. DNA-CuNCs have been applied to detect various analytes, such as hydrogen peroxide [20], protein [21], exonuclease III [22], and polynucleotide kinase [23]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there has not been any report on the application of DNA-CuNCs for NACs determination.

Considering the electron deficient ability of TNP and taking advantage of the desirable optical property of DNA-CuNCs, herein, we developed a simple, reliable, and sensitive fluorescent strategy for the specific recognition of TNP in aqueous solutions. In the proposed sensing strategy, poly-T DNA was used as both the template for CuNCs formation and the electron-rich groups reacting with the proton donating target, TNP. Upon the addition of TNP, the resultant DNA-CuNCs-TNP complex shows a significant fluorescence decrease, which is ascribed to the synergetic effects of acid induction and photo electron transfer between TNP and DNA-CuNCs. The fluorescence intensity of DNA-CuNCs in aqueous solution decreases along with the increase of TNP concentration, applied as a readout signal for constructing fluorescent platform for TNP sensing. It was also found that other NACs analogues including TNT, DNT, DNP, NP, NB, BP, and BT showed little contribution to the fluorescence decrease, implying the outstanding selectivity of proposed method for TNP detection. Compared with the previously reported works that commonly involved complex DNA sequence design, fluorescence dye labels, and sophisticated organic reactions, our strategy processes the advantages of high sensitivity, good selectivity, easy preparation, low toxicity, and excellent optical property.

HPLC-purified poly(thymine), T50 with the sequence of 5′-T50–3′, was purchased from Sangon Biotech Company, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 3-(N-morpholino) propanesulfonic acid (MOPS), copper sulfate, sodium chloride, sodium ascorbate, and other salts were obtained from Dingguo Biotechnology Company, Ltd. (Beijing, China). 2,4,6-trinitrophenol, 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene, 2,4-dinitrotoluene, 2,4-dinitrophenol, 3-nitrophenol, nitrobenzene, phenol, and toluene were purchased from Energy Chemical Company, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification or treatment. Ultra-pure water (resistivity >18.2MΩcm @25°C) obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore Corp., Bedford, MA, USA) was used throughout the experiments.

All fluorescence measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer (Japan) equipped with a xenon lamp as the excitation source and a personal computer as the data processing unit. The slits for excitation and emission were set at 5.0nm and 10.0nm, respectively, and the excitation voltage was 700V. The excitation wavelength was set at 340nm, and the emission spectra from 520nm to 660nm were collected. The fluorescence intensity at 627nm was used to evaluate the performance of the proposed TNP sensing strategy. UV-Visible absorption spectra were recorded on a UV/Vis/NIR 2600 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) using quartz cuvettes with 1cm path length. Gel electrophoresis images were obtained on a Bio-Rad Gel Doc XR+system (Hercules, CA, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a HT7700 microscope (Hitachi, Japan) operated at 100kV. The TEM specimens were made by placing a drop of the nanoparticles suspension on a carbon-coated copper grid.

DNA-CuNCs were synthesized according to the reported method [19]. In a typical procedure, the stock solutions of Poly T were first diluted to desired concentration in MOPS buffer (10mM MOPS, 150mM NaCl, pH 7.4). Then, sodium ascorbate and copper sulfate were added into the poly T solutions, reacting for certain time. The final volume of the solution was brought to 100μL with MOPS buffer, and the fluorescent signal was measured.

The feasibility investigation of TNP sensing was first carried out by detecting TNP dissolved in water. Typically, 90μL of the synthesized DNA-CuNCs aqueous solution and 10μL of TNP with different concentrations were incubated for 30min at room temperature. The fluorescence signals of the mixtures were subsequently recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TNP in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The artificial lake water samples were obtained from Qingdao Agriculture University campus. The samples were centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 20min twice and then the supernatant was filtered using 0.22µm water-phase microfiltration membranes. A 50μL aliquot of the treated water sample spiked with different concentrations of TNP was added into DNA-CuNCs solution. The final volume of the resulting solution was added to 100μL with MOPS buffer. The fluorescence spectra of the solutions were collected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of fluorescence TNP biosensing strategy
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrates the general principle of the fluorescence biosensor for TNP detection based on the synergetic quenching effects of acid induction and electron transfer. Upon the addition of Cu2+ and ascorbate to poly-T, DNA-templated copper nanoclusters (DNA-CuNCs) were formed, which emitted fluorescence with the maximum emission wavelength of 627nm. The synthesized DNA-CuNCs also exhibited excellent optical stability, whose fluorescence intensity changed little upon fabrication and stored in air for dozens of minutes. In the presence of TNP, the donor-acceptor complexes between TNP and DNA-CuNCs were formed and the self assembling of DNA-CuNCs was further induced, which quenched the luminescence of DNA-CuNCs. Additionally, the acidity of TNP can also decrease the fluorescence intensity of the prepared DNA-CuNCs. The reduced fluorescence intensity can be regarded as a readout signal for the sensing process. However, upon the addition of other explosives, such as TNT, DNT, DNP, NP, NB, BP, and BT, DNA-CuNCs could not recognize the explosives to form no donor-acceptor complexes, and the acidity of the explosives was not strong enough to depress the fluorescence intensity of DNA-CuNCs. Therefore, highly sensitive and selective detection of TNP can be easily achieved by using the as-proposed DNA-CuNCs-based fluorescence sensing strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility study of fluorescent TNP assay
ce:section.ce:para: To illustrate the feasibility of the proposed strategy, the proof-of-concept experiments were carried out for TNP detection. As shown in Fig. 1, upon the fabrication of fluorescent CuNCs using poly-T DNA as the templates, high fluorescence intensity was observed with the maximum emission wavelength centered at 627nm (line a). However, when TNP was added into the system, a significant decrease of the fluorescence intensity was obtained (line b). Under such conditions, the donor-accepter complex would form for the strong interaction between poly-T DNA and TNP, leading to a close proximity between them. Because of the electron transfer effect and the strong acidity of TNP, the fluorescence of DNA-CuNCs would be quenched, resulting in low fluorescence intensity. In addition, the photostability of DNA-CuNCs was also characterized. It is found that no evident fluorescence decline was observed during a one-hour period after the fabrication, with the intensity at about 257.2a.u. (see Fig. S1 in Supplementary material), indicating that the as-prepared DNA-CuNCs possess excellent optical property in aqueous solution. These results demonstrated the feasibility of the as-proposed DNA-CuNCs-based fluorescence strategy for TNP detection.

Since the pH value, the reaction time, and the concentration of sodium ascorbate have evident influence on the sensing signal, in order to obtain the best performance, these experimental conditions were optimized.

As illustrated in Fig. S2A (in Supplementary material), when other conditions were kept unchanged, the fluorescence intensity increased dramatically as the pH value of the system increased from 4.0 to 6.8, and decreased significantly as the pH increased from 8.5 to 13. However, in the pH range of 6.8–8.5, the fluorescence intensity barely changed, maintaining a maximum value. Thus, considering the conditions of DNA-CuNCs formation and the fluorescence stability, 7.4 was chosen as the optimal pH value. Fig. S2B (in Supplementary material) shows that, with the reaction time of TNP and DNA-CuNCs changed from 0 to 50min, initially the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly, and then leveled off with the reaction time longer than 30min. Therefore, a reaction time of 30min was adopted in the following experiments.

It could be seen from Fig. S2C (in Supplementary Material) that the fluorescence intensity increased remarkably with the sodium ascorbate concentration increased from 0 to 4.0mM, indicating that sodium ascorbate acted as an efficient reducing agent in the formation of CuNCs. TNP, with three electron-withdrawing nitro groups on the benzene ring, is prone to react with reducing agents violently. Thus, in the proposed strategy, to prevent the interference of sodium ascorbate on TNP sensing, the concentration of sodium ascorbate cannot be too high. Whereas, when the concentration of sodium ascorbate was lower than 2.0mM, the fluorescence intensity was too low to provide enough sensitivity for TNP detection. Therefore, 2.0mM was chosen as the optimal sodium ascorbate concentration and used in the subsequent experiments.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, TNP with different concentrations was added into the DNA-CuNCs system to evaluate the analytical performance of this fluorescent sensing platform. As illustrated in Fig. 2A, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with the increase of TNP concentration, which was in accordance with the fact that TNP with higher concentration would quench more DNA-CuNCs because of the synergetic quenching effects of acid induction and electron transfer. The fluorescence quenching efficiency was analyzed using the Stern-Volmer equation I0/I=1+Ksv·[M], where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities before and after adding TNP, respectively, and Ksv is the quenching coefficient. Fig. S3 (in Supplementary Material) shows the relative fluorescence intensity (I0/I) vs. TNP concentration in aqueous solution. The Ksv was calculated to be 1.15×104 Lmol−1, implying perfect quenching ability.

Fig. 2B shows a good linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity of DNA-CuNCs and the TNP concentration ranging from 0.1 to 100μM. The correlation equation was determined to be F=−0.7508C+256.2 (F, fluorescence intensity, a.u.; C, TNP concentration, μM), with a correlation coefficient of R2=0.9969. The detection limit for TNP was estimated to be 0.03μM (based on signal-to-noise ratio of 3), which is lower than those obtained using the previously reported organic or polymer fluorescent materials. Thus, the results demonstrated that highly sensitive fluorescence detection of TNP can be successfully realized using the as-proposed approach.

The specificity of the as-proposed detection method was further investigated by substituting TNP with seven other explosives, namely 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT), 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP), 3-nitrophenol (NP), nitrobenzene (NB), phenol (BP), and toluene (BT), and their chemical structures are presented in Fig. S4 (in Supplementary material). As can be seen from Fig. 3, significantly decreased fluorescence signal was observed only in the presence of the target explosive TNP. The fluorescence intensities were very strong in the presence of TNT, DNT, DNP, NP, NB, BP, or BT, comparable to that in the control experiment. For the fluorescent DNA-CuNCs, the Ksv for TNT, DNT, DNP, NB, and NP are 48.4, 60.7, 234.5, 35.6, and 64Lmol−1, respectively. In particular, the quenching constant of DNA-CuNCs for TNP is at least 49 times greater than those for TNT and other nitroaromatic explosives, showing good selectivity for the detection of the target TNP. Therefore, these results are in accordance with the as-proposed sensing principle and demonstrate good potential of the method for selective TNP explosive sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of TNP sensor in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate its applicability the as-proposed DNA-CuNCs fluorescent sensor was employed to detect TNP in water samples. As clearly shown in Table 1, there was good agreement between the added and the measured values of TNP concentration, and the recoveries were found to be in the range of 106%–115%, suggesting no severe interferences in such samples. Moreover, the relative standard deviations (RSD) of three repetitive measurements for each sample were all below 7%, demonstrating a relatively high reproducibility. These results indicate that the DNA-CuNCs fluorescent sensor has good reliability and reproducibility, and can be successfully applied for the detection of TNP in water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for selective response of DNA-CuNCs to TNP
ce:section.ce:para: There may be a special interaction between DNA-CuNCs and TNP, which gives rise to the obvious difference between TNP and other NACs on quenching the fluorescence emission. By considering the structural characteristics of both DNA-CuNCs and TNP, a probable mechanism based on the synergetic quenching effects of acid induction and electron transfer is presented to interpret the exceptional selective response of the fluorescent material to TNP.

As is generally known, TNP is a highly electron deficient molecule, easily interacted with electron-rich compounds. In contrast, DNA-CuNCs exhibit excellent electron-rich property due to a multitude of lone-pair electron containing nitrogen atoms in their DNA moieties on the surface of CuNCs. When TNP was added into the DNA-CuNCs solution, the thymines on the surface of CuNCs are expected to capture the protons in the hydroxyl groups of TNP molecules. Consequently, TNP can be effectively bound to the surface of CuNCs, further quenching the fluorescence through the electron transfer effect. In addition, no spectral overlap between the absorption of TNP and the emission of DNA-CuNCs was observed (see Fig. S5 in Supplementary material), which implies that no energy transfer exists from the excited state of DNA-CuNCs to the ground state of TNP. Therefore, the selective fluorescence quenching response of DNA-CuNCs to TNP can be explained by the formation of donor-accepter complex and the resulting photo electron transfer effect. To further confirm the electron transfer-induced quenching mechanism, TEM images of DNA-CuNCs before and after adding TNP were obtained. As illustrated in Fig. 4, the addition of TNP induced the aggregation of the originally dispersive DNA-CuNCs, which indicated that fluorescent DNA-CuNCs could be brought closer to TNP, quenching the fluorescence through the electron transfer effect. Furthermore, the gel electrophoresis analysis was carried out to characterize the interaction process. Fig. S6 (in Supplementary material) depicts that no obvious band difference was observed between DNA, DNA-CuNCs, and DNA-CuNCs-TNP samples, which further confirmed that the fluorescence decrease is mainly caused by the electron transfer-induced quenching effect resulted from the donor-accepter interaction between DNA-CuNCs and TNP, not by the changes of DNA strands.

In addition to the electron transfer effect, the acidity of TNP also contributes to the quenching of the fluorescence of DNA-CuNCs. As previously shown in Fig. 2A, the fluorescence intensity of DNA-CuNCs decreased significantly as the solution pH was reduced from 6.8 to 4.0. As is also well known, TNP can be regarded as a strong protonic acid due to the bonding of three electron-withdrawing nitro groups on the benzene ring. When TNP was added into the DNA-CuNCs solution, the pH of the solution would decrease, and the acidity would increase, further quenching the fluorescence of DNA-CuNCs. To further gain the insight into the mechanism for specific recognition of TNP, we carried out a comparative experiment. As clearly illustrated in Fig. S7 (in Supplementary material), the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly in the presence of TNP under neutral condition (line a), or in the absence of TNP under basic condition (pH 11) (line b); interestingly, when other conditions were kept unchanged, a significantly enhanced fluorescence intensity was observed in the presence of TNP under basic condition (line c), which could be because the hydroxyl groups of TNP were deprotonated in the basic system to reduce its acidity, thus, the acidity-induced fluorescence quenching efficiency was decreased.

In summary, by taking advantage of its simplicity in synthesis and good optical properties, DNA-CuNCs has been successfully exploited as an effective signal indicator for constructing a novel fluorescent strategy for selective TNP sensing. The sensing mechanism is mainly based on the synergetic effects of acidity induction and electron transfer between DNA-CuNCs and TNP, which possesses high electron deficiency and strong protonic acidity. Highly sensitive TNP detection has been realized, with a directly measured detection limit down to 0.03 μM, which is lower than those reported by using organic fluorescent materials. Moreover, the as-proposed strategy demonstrates a highly specific recognition for TNP over other NACs. This sensing strategy exhibits the advantages of simplicity, cost-effectiveness, high sensitivity and outstanding selectivity, and has been successfully adopted for fluorescent detection of TNP in natural water samples, indicating its great potential to be applied in the fields of homeland security and environmental monitoring. This strategy can also be used to detect other targets which can cause the changes of the metal core, thus expanding the application of DNA-CuNCs-based label-free optical assays and sensing to a variety of fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21575074, 21375072 and 21545005), the Basic Research Program of Qingdao (No. 14-2-4-102-jch), the Research Foundation for Distinguished Scholars of Qingdao Agricultural University (Nos. 663-1113311 and 663-1113334), and the Special Foundation for Taishan Scholar of Shandong Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at 10.1016/j.talanta.2016.07.030.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material

.
